replies.author: @Cwin-ny6bp
replies.text: Your thoughts kinda mirror mine. Objectively, there is a lot to criticize. However, it’s kinda rare that some big cultural sensation show that runs for years doesn’t totally bollocks the ending or fall off completely (looking at you Lost, Heroes, The Walking Dead, and Game of Thrones among others). While I can and certainly do criticize some of the decisions here, it was largely satisfying and actually both tied up most of the major plot points and explained the “why?” of everything to a satisfactory but not overdone extent. I also enjoyed the redemption fakeout with Henry, where it was revealed that he had been corrupted but was very much a willing participant instead of a pawn. It was a good example of not giving a redemption arc to a character who doesn’t deserve redemption.

But the Duffers story writing really has some plot holes. The show could have been much better than it was if they:
1. Stopped catering to "fanfiction" audience. 
2. Actually tried to make a story that fleshes out every detail. So many plot lines were opened just to be left untouched.

So much wasted potential.

replies.author: @christianclark347
replies.text:
I played in mid to late 90s and even then , yeah, if any girls gave it any thought at all it was to call the people who played it weird and creepy. The nerdy girls at the time simply weren't interested, they mostly stuck to various shoujo animes like Sailor Moon or similar things to that. 3 and 3.5 were first and foremost combat simulators with much less emphasis on roleplaying and personal interaction than the current systems seem to have.

I briefly considered getting back into it a few years back but all the amateur play-acting going on (and even being encouraged in the rulebooks) really threw me off. In the old days, attempting any of that was considered a cardinal sin or at least supremely cringe-worthy . You dictated what your character was doing, you never acted him out. But I get it, times change. I just see no need to change my tastes with them, especially in matters of leisure.

replies.text:
@lyndy101 Not possible in 2025.  Don't you know that all fathers must be depicted as ignorant, useless, and uncaring, or a figure of ridicule, if they even bother to depict them at all?  Where's Dustin's father?  Mike's dad is a buffoon.  Will's dad is just a useless, selfish grifter who abandoned his family.  Max's father is a violent, dangerous brute, just like his son.  Eleven's father is completely absent, abandoning El's mother and leaving the child at the mercy of the creepy and evil father-figure of Dr. Brenner.  Naturally she never shows the slightest interest in discovering anything about who her true father was!  Lucas actually _does_ have a father, though he appears only briefly to tell his son that his mother is always right about everything.  He's the only one who isn't portrayed as despicable (by feminist standards) or absent, and you can guess why that might be.  Naturally all the heroic roles are given to single moms, or women whose husbands are so defective that they might as well be.
Exactly as it was in the 1980s!

replies.text:
I 100% agree. The cast was too big from the start, but they continued to add more, without killing anyone off. The three characters they did kill off, were three of the best, and most interesting. 

Will, Robin, the annoying af black girl, and more importantly, one of the boys, preferably Mike, should've been killed off! Not only to trim the cast down, but to prove the good guys might not live through it. At no point did it ever feel like anyone was in any danger.

They added and focused on characters we didn't need to see. It was supposed to be the final series, not building to the next one. Derek was pretty good, the kidnapping tangent, annoying Holly, wasn't needed at all. They killed off the Black army dude, just to replace him with the same character now played by Linda Hamilton. I mean, an old female general, remember it's set in the 80s, meaning she served in WWII,  isn't very believable.

Bringing back the Indian girl from the least popular moment of the entire series, wasn't the best move, either! 

Adding another sand dimension, which was as bland as it could possibly be, just wasn't needed, looked boring and had zero risks. Not a good replacement for the upsidedown. 

They messed up by killing off Papa, twice! He was a great antagonist. If he was still alive, they could've given Henry the chance to make up for what he did to him, teamed up with him, or simply have him manipulate everyone and everything, whatever, it would've been something.

Season 4 was on to something. Henry was a great character, however, I don't want to admit it, but El just ended up being an all powerful, invincible Mary Sue, after all. It did look like she had a character arc, but actually she didn't. She beat Henry with relative ease, every time. Resulting in a foregone finale. They should've played it as El beat Henry because he was off guard, the first time, but no, she beat him easily when she was a little kid. 

Then, instead of manipulative and or powerful antagonists, we had a brainless roaring kaiju. 

Stranger Things, had some great moments, mostly in series 1 and 4, and overall it was good, but it unfortunately ended on a cheesy, gay, anticlimactic downer. I hated Nancy from the start. I don't want to be mean, but she should've been played by an attractive actress. I didn't mind Robin at first, but I hated seeing her 40 year old face in series 5! What an annoying character!

Second of all. It is known through studies that each next child of the same sex from one mother is more likely to be homosexual, at 7th child of the same sex it's almost guaranteed. So homosexuality is a natural form of quantity control in population. Plus, remember that resources are finite, thus bigger population means higher competition for resources. In social species, which humans are, homosexual specimen still produce/procure said resources, food and goods etc. But since they're less likely to produce their own offspring, the excess of the resources accumulated by them goes not to their children but to children of their siblings or to those who need it in the pack/community, if homosexual specimen doesn't have any siblings. 
Also, if a specimen who has offspring dies, or for some other reason can't take care of their offspring, homosexual specimen can and do replace them and take care of other's offspring, adopt it. I haven't mentioned yet that homosexual specimen on average are less aggressive towards other specimen's offspring and far less likely to do the things you or someone else mentioned above. For all the same reasons I've already mentioned.

Homosexuality is a functional evolutionary mechanism, just as much as dimorphism is.
But I guess you don't really care about evolution and evolutionary benefits, do you? Otherwise you would've known it by now.

author: @someguy3508
text:
As someone who has watched the entire show in 2 weeks for the first time, I can tell that the show has not lost its character and it's been consistent with the writing. I think people put ST way higher than it actually is because it was something new and exciting. The truth is, things have always worked for the main cast and they never did anything special to solve problems. From the start I was like "really? That's how this is solved?", but I pushed through because this show is beloved. I liked S5 and I think Will's coming out is on brand with the character and the series. 
It has been consistent. The truth is that the show never explained stuff. We still don't know how Brenner found this new dimension and wormhole. We don't know why Henry killed his sister (he explained about his dad and mom, and we know he doesn't kill without reason), we don't know how the hell the Russians entered the US and built and entire underground facility beneath Hawkins without anyone noticing and then kidnapping Hopper and the Demon dogs without anyone noticing anything. The main cast are all great spies, fighters, scientists and detectives. 

What I'm trying to say is that ST is just a kid's show with elements of gore, horror and jump scares. It's a fun show that you can turn your mind off and enjoy. It's not complex and it's not really that well written. Some characters are good and the theme and idea are amazing. Imo these attributes carry the show and especially Dustin and Steve did a lot of the weightlifting. Their characters and relationship are a masterpiece. Eddie was also amazing and tbh this is where ST excelled. Character interactions. The plot and story could improve a lot

replies.text: ​@ethandowler4669 I have a feeling this "could have been worse" mentality comes from years of disappointments and high-profile flops. It feels like just "clearing the bar," no matter how messily, is considered good enough. And even though there were some good moments in the finale, for a series with *this* much hype and cultural impact, especially with how long it takes between seasons, it didn’t live up to expectations at all. It's not a complete dumpster fire, but I can totally understand why people would consider Stranger Things season 5 a flop.

replies.author: @gpv-n
replies.text:
@Mastordant The 'building' part of Will is just about the tunnels under Hawkins, not the creation of the wormhole.
What maintains the wormhole open is the presence of exotic matter; the unexplained part is how it spawned there (if a natural occurrence) or who placed it (if artificially created).
Dustin suggests the bridge was created when El made contact with the other world, but that doesn't really explain the exotic matter part, or why El was able to break through in the first place. 
But the whole writers' attempt to exhaustively rationalize all the parts of the story & events is just shaky anyway. It's blatantly obvious that all of it are afterthoughts patched together.

replies.author: @Randomusername56782
@FullEnglishYT
replies.text:
​@Randomusername56782 but the difference with Afghanistan and Vietnam is that they weren't secrets. In Stranger Things, all those soldiers who died whilst "there to help with the earthquake", could quite easily be written off as victims of a single tragic helicopter crash or similar as a cover-up. Afghanistan and Vietnam were never secrets, but even if they were, the volume of casualties could never be covered up anywhere near so simply. If the government can get away with the wider public never knowing at all, I absolutely believe they would seize that opportunity. 

Imagine your nephew Mike, his mom, dad and two sisters all go missing. You obviously don't give up looking into their disspaearance. You find out they are in prison for a multitude of reasons including killing soldiers. The official explanation is they are just a family of psychos and, for no reason at all, we're involved in killing soldiers unprovoked... even little Holly. Would you look into it further? Why were the soldiers there? Why were they were saving children who had been abducted by soldiers? Saving them from monsters with telekinetic powers? Saving everyone from the end of the world?

Surely the more logical solution would be an agreement that no action is taken as long as nothing ever comes out about it. The only other logical solution would be to kill them all to ensure their secrecy. Trials and prosecutions would serve no benefit for anyone but if they kill them all off, there would be no point to the TV show at all.

text:
All that time establishing a connection with Will and the Upside Down, and you never even turn him into a hero? I'm stunned that they didn't. If they had actually planned this out, then Will's powers should have appeared much sooner, and the arc would have naturally become he is brave enough to sacrifice himself for everyone he loves, but especially to save Mike, which in turn would give Mike an entirely different perspective on what it takes to love someone.

So many missed opportunities for keeping-friendships-in-the-industry-intact cash grabbing. If only they'd of all had the guts to be more.

replies.author: @gustavventer7696
replies.text: I completely agree with you. If you ask me, Will should’ve gotten his powers at the end of season 3. Then, in season 4, El trains him to use his powers, and in season 5, episode 4 becomes the breaking point for Will, where he reaches his full potential (or at least something like that). This way, I feel we would’ve had a proper build-up for Will’s powers. Instead, his powers feel too rushed, too strong, and too convenient. Will sacrificing himself would’ve made a much bigger and more meaningful difference in the plot of the series. As sad as that would’ve been, it would’ve worked really well. But yeah, I agree—so many missed opportunities. The worst part for me is that The Duffer Brothers had two or three years to write season 5, and this is what they came up with.

author: @invox_in_your_inbox
text:
Here's how I think it should have ended:
They got to the Abyss, free the children and fight Vecna. At the last minute, Eleven happears and kills him impailing him on that spike. Will's mother gives the final blow, but it's just ONE swing with all the moments she suffered in ONE montage flashback (we didn't had to see all that gore). After that, while leaving the lair, the Mindflair awakens with Henry's mind fused with it. Eleven tries to fight it but she's too weak for something that big. While this is happening, he calls all the Demos (yeah, remember those guys), so the gang has to fight them off to protect the kids.
And this is where all those kids come into play, they chanel their minds through Will to help El (he's "kinda" Vecna anyway). The gang fight of the Demos and WILL charges up El to super-sayan and she kills the Mindflair. When this happens the Demos regain their freewill and just leave/flee. They then leave the upside down in the usual ending... with one diference: Eleven remains dead.
The way I would tell the "explanation" would be to have Kali appears in a short-montage of friends Eleven is trying to protect and giving her strenght to destroys the mindflair (thus  having to move said "montage" from Will's mom to El, has not to have two of the same in one episode). SO in the first instance, the audience thinks it's a memory from EL, but in the explanation we perceive that (maybe) it was an indication that Kali was actually still alive while Eleven was fighting in the Abyss, thus creating an ambiguous moment for the viewer to decide which one to believe, like they did.

That said, I really liked the ending. My sole disappointment was the underuse of Linda's character. She does VERY little in the show and that was a big let down. She could: have explained where the Midflair artfact (from Henry's memory), how the wormhole was build, and specially, WHY those couldn't be recreated, and even why they'd be no repercussion for all those dead soldiers. IN MY MIND, she was actually Henry's mother(?) and she had a WHOLE lot more at stake in this than what was showed. Also, I was thinking that Henry's fear of the cave was much simpler. I though he was afraid of scorpion's and that's how that related to the Midflair (since he does look like a big scorpio). He might had fallen down a shaft and be covered in scorpions, that sort of thing.
Anyway, that's is just me... glad you read this far. Despite it all, this was my 2nd favourite season (after the 1st) and I really enjoyed it. Cheers!
